The director needs to arrange the scenes in such a way that no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade. The release years of the movies are 1994, 2004, 2007, 2012, and 2015. These fall into the decades {1990-1999}, {2000-2009}, and {2010-2019} respectively. The shooting days required are 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 days. To minimize the total number of shooting days while following the constraint, the optimal sequence is 1994, 2012, 2004, 2015, 2007. This sequence alternates between the decades and uses all the scenes with the least total days. The total number of shooting days is 4 + 10 + 6 + 12 + 8 = 40. The sequence ensures that no two consecutive scenes are from the same decade. The total number of shooting days is minimized. The director can shoot the scenes in this sequence to achieve the goal.  
******  
The director has a budget of ₹50 lakhs and needs to maximize the number of completed scenes. The cost per day for each scene is ₹1 lakh, ₹1.5 lakhs, ₹2 lakhs, ₹2.5 lakhs, and ₹3 lakhs respectively. Let x1, x2, x3, x4, x5 represent the number of days each scene is shot. The objective is to maximize the number of scenes completed, which is equivalent to maximizing the number of non-zero x values. The constraint is that the total cost ∑(cost per day * number of days) ≤ 50 lakhs. The linear programming problem can be formulated as: maximize the number of non-zero x values subject to ∑(cost per day * x) ≤ 50. The maximum number of scenes that can be completed is 4 by choosing scenes with lower costs. The optimal solution involves shooting scenes with costs of ₹1, ₹1.5, ₹2, and ₹2.5 lakhs for 4, 6, 8, and 10 days respectively, totaling 40 lakhs. The remaining 10 lakhs can be used for additional days on one of the scenes
